The main entrance of the temple is on the east, but visitors can also enter from the west or the north. According to the Preah Khan inscription, there were as many as 20,400 statues of Buddha, Shiva, and Vishnu in the temple, crafted in gold, silver, bronze, and stone (though George Cœdès reported only 430 divine images). title: The ancient complex of Preah Khan Kompong Svay
highlights: It is a large Khmer temple complex  located in Sangkum Thmei commune, Preah Vihear province, approximately 105km to the east of Angkor. The temple is well known for three things ; firstly it was a Mahayana-Buddhism temple related the concept of  ...    in each corner,  similar  to that found at East Mebon temple in Angkor. Moreover, the evidence of Buddhism is found on the lintel of this complex, which is related to the concept of ‘pure  ...    Archaeological remains found in situ, are physical evidence of  a model of a satellite city in the Khmer empire. These can be seen clearly uniqueness of concept via the well-designed urban planning, especially the interaction between various  types of remains, the ancient road, iron industryl remains, and religious elements. The cluster of industrial remain (iron kilns) inside the ancient complex conveys a long history of metallurgy in Cambodia. It believed that the  ... title: Buddhist Study in Preah Khan of Kompong Svay | The Two Buddhist Towers of Preah Khan of Kompong Svay
highlights:
its practice and impact through the pre-Angkorian to Angkorian periods remain substantially muted due in large part to the relative infrequency of specific temple dedications and discussion within the epigraphic sources.  ...    epigraphic and material evidence. Innovative and comprehensive investigations in this model have been applied in India.  ...    Identifying evidence of Buddhism, however, is complicated by the fact that the majority of preserved temples dedicated to the religion during the Angkorian period are restricted to the late 12th to 13th centuries and that the switch to Theravada did not produce such grandiose masonry architecture and state-level infrastructure. The lone site that may hold the key to elucidating the manifold aspects of Buddhism within Khmer history is Preah Khan of Kompong Svay. ### Interesting links

title: Preah Khan History
highlights: Preah Khan was built on the site ofJayavarman VII‘s victory over the invadingChamsin 1191. Unusually the modern name, meaning “holy sword”, is derived from the meaning of the original—*Nagara Jayasri*(holy city of victory).[[1]]The site may previously have been occupied by the royal palaces ofYasovarman IIandTribhuvanadityavarman.[[2]]The temple’s foundationstelahas provided considerable information about the history and administration of the site: the main image, of thebodhisattvaAvalokiteśvarain the form of the king’s father, was dedicated in 1191 (the king’s mother had earlier been commemorated in the same way atTa Prohm). 430 other deities also had shrines on the site, each of which received an allotment of food,  ...    silver,gems, 112,300 pearls and acowwithgildedhorns.[[4]]The institution combined the roles of city, temple and Buddhist university: there were 97,840 attendants and servants, including 1000 dancers[[5]]and 1000 teachers.[[6]] The temple is still largely

title: Preah Khan in Angkor - ANGKOR TEMPLES IN CAMBODIA
highlights: In 1191 Preah Khan was dedicated to Jayavarman VII's father, the central statue was called "Jayavarmeshvara", meaning "Jayavarman, Lord of the world". Jayavarman's father was worshipped as a personification of the universal Bodhisattva  ...    as Prajnaparamita, female embodiment of perfect wisdom.
